Today's Story

We follow Asheville's Airbnb market through supply, demand, revenue, performance, and guest voice.

Act I · Market Supply
Where Does
Asheville Host?
With 5,500+ active listings across 8 neighborhoods, the real question isn't where they are — it's who's running them and how professionally.
Most Listings
Asheville West
~1,600 active listings
Highest Priced
Arden
~$220 avg nightly rate (entire home)
Downtown Private Rooms
$200+
Average nightly rate — the professional hub
Act I · Supply — Interactive Map
Listings Density by Neighborhood
Hover a neighborhood to see listing count, avg price & rating · Darker = more listings
Most Listings
Asheville West
~1,600 active listings
Act I · Supply
West & Downtown Dominate Supply
Asheville West (~1,600) and Downtown/Central (~1,200) account for nearly half of all listings. The question isn't where — it's who's operating them.

High-supply neighborhoods attract both casual and professional hosts. Disentangling those two groups is where the market story begins.

Act II · Market Demand
How Busy Is
the Market — Really?
Occupancy tells a more nuanced story. Seasonal peaks, a decade of growth, and Hurricane Helene all leave fingerprints in the data.
Market Shock
Occupancy dropped from 59% to 29% in three months following Hurricane Helene (Sep 2024)
Professional hosts with dynamic pricing and high acceptance rates were better positioned to capture remaining demand during the downturn.
Act II · Demand
Occupancy Fell Hard After Helene — But Is Recovering
The market entered fall 2025 at ~59% occupancy. Helene triggered a contraction to ~29% by January 2026. By summer 2026, climbing back toward 48%.

Professional hosts with dynamic pricing and high acceptance rates captured whatever demand remained during the downturn.

Act II · Demand
Occupancy: Distribution & Market Trend
Median occupancy is 28% — the bimodal distribution reveals a bifurcated market of struggling casual hosts and fully-booked professionals
Act II · Demand · Time Series
A Decade of Seasonality — And Two Shocks
STL decomposition shows long-run growth, reliable annual cycles, and anomalous shocks from COVID-19 and Hurricane Helene
Act II · Demand · Forecast
SARIMA Says: Uncertain But Optimistic Recovery
A SARIMA(1,1,1)(1,1,1)[12] model projects gradual review volume recovery through 2026. Wide confidence intervals reflect the novelty of the Helene shock.

Hosts who maintain pricing discipline and listing quality during the recovery will be best positioned when demand normalizes.

Act III · Revenue
What Are Hosts
Actually Earning?
The gap between top and bottom earners tracks with operational sophistication, not just room type or address.
Revenue Gap
3.5×
Hotel-style operators vs typical entire home hosts
Act III · Revenue
Hotel Rooms Generate 3.5× More — Because They're Run Like Businesses
Hotel rooms: ~$3,700 median monthly revenue vs ~$1,000 for entire homes. The gap reflects professional management, dynamic pricing, and consistently high occupancy.

The hotel room model is a blueprint for professional hosting: treat the listing as a business, not a side project.

Act III · Revenue · Seasonality
Revenue Seasonality Differs Dramatically by Room Type
Entire homes peak in fall and summer; hotel rooms maintain a revenue floor through active yield management; casual hosts show the sharpest winter troughs
Act III · Revenue · Geography
Neighborhood Matters — But Professionals Extract Value Everywhere
Arden leads entire home pricing (~$220); Downtown leads private rooms ($200+). The spread within each neighborhood is wide — professional hosts consistently outprice their neighbors.
Core Finding
"
Asheville's top earners aren't lucky with location — they're professionals with systems.
#1+2
Price & Host Scale — top occupancy drivers
3.5×
Revenue gap, pro vs casual operators
0.072
Acceptance Rate — a systems metric
0.85
Avg sentiment — held through both shocks
Act IV · Performance Drivers
What Actually
Moves the Needle?
A Random Forest regression on listing attributes surfaces the true occupancy drivers — and almost all of them are within a host's control.
Model
Random Forest Regression
Trained on listing-level attributes · Top 8 features shown by importance score
Key Result
6 of 8 drivers are host-controlled
Pricing, scale, review scores, acceptance rate — all learnable behaviors
Act IV · Random Forest
Six of the Top Eight Drivers Are Things Hosts Control
Price (0.134) and Host Listings Count (0.133) lead. Review scores for value, location, cleanliness, and overall rating all in top eight. Acceptance Rate (0.072) confirms responsiveness drives bookings.

This is a host behavior scorecard. Price competitively, accept consistently, maintain cleanliness — and occupancy follows.

Act V · Guest Voice
What Do 100,000
Reviews Say?
VADER sentiment analysis and LDA topic modeling transform unstructured reviews into structured market intelligence about what guests value most.
Avg Sentiment
0.85
Compound VADER score across all neighborhoods
Top LDA Topic
Asheville Location & Walkability
Dominant across all 8 neighborhoods
Act V · LDA Topic Modeling
Six Topics Emerge from Guest Reviews
Guests write about mountains, amenities, space, location, host care, and urban access — "Host Thoughtfulness & Extras" directly reflects professional hosting behavior
Act V · Topic Distribution
Location Dominates — But Host Thoughtfulness Gets the Best Sentiment
Most reviews center on location, but "Host Thoughtfulness & Extras" scores 0.801 sentiment — third highest. Professional hosts generate this topic through repeatable systems.

Not personality — process. Welcome baskets, local guides, fast communication. Repeatable and learnable by any host.

Act V · Topics by Neighborhood
Topic Mix Is Consistent Across Neighborhoods
No single neighborhood dominates a topic — what guests write about reflects host behavior and Asheville's brand, not just which zip code they stayed in
Act V · VADER Sentiment
Sentiment Is Uniformly High Across All Neighborhoods
Scores range 0.838–0.865 — a narrow band confirming quality is driven by host behavior, not neighborhood. Negative reviews are under 2% everywhere.
Act V · Sentiment × Topic
Amenity Quality Is the One Weak Spot — And It's Fixable
Mountain Views (0.951) and Location (0.854) lead. Amenity Quality scores just 0.044 — hot tubs described as old, photos not matching reality, missing pool access.

Solvable problem. Professional hosts audit their amenity promises and fix gaps systematically — casual hosts don't.

Act V · Sentiment Over Time
Quality Held Through Every Shock — A Professional Standard
Sentiment stayed between 0.83–0.87 from 2019–2025. Neither COVID-19 nor Helene produced a lasting decline — hosts who kept operating maintained their standards.

Resilient sentiment during demand shocks is a hallmark of professional operations — systems don't fail just because volume drops.

Synthesis
The Professional
Host Playbook
Every lens — supply, demand, revenue, ML, NLP — points to the same conclusion: the host, not the home, is the decisive variable.
Headline Finding
"Asheville's top earners aren't lucky with location — they're professionals with systems."
1
Price and scale are the top two occupancy driversHost Listings Count (0.133) is tied with Price (0.134) — professional multi-listing operators systematically outperform casual hosts.
2
The revenue gap is structural, not geographicHotel-style operators earn 3.5× more through operational discipline — dynamic pricing, high acceptance rates, consistent reviews.
3
Amenity quality is the clearest improvement targetLowest sentiment topic (0.044) driven by gaps between listed and actual amenities. Professional hosts audit and fix systematically.
4
Sentiment resilience through Helene proves the modelHosts who maintained quality held their ratings. The market will recover — professional operators will be ready for it.
